sectionHeader.title: What is ADHD Coaching?
sectionHeader.subtitle: A thousand colorful bricks. One puzzling thought.

subsections.title: Prologue: A View from the Rug

defaultContent.text: You’re sitting on the floor, surrounded by a thousand colorful bricks. You know exactly what you want to build — a 1,254-piece masterpiece. (Well, 1,251. You lost three pieces somewhere.)

You've wanted to build for weeks. You opened the box three times before. Today is the day!
But you don't start. You just sit there. Completely aware of everything you need to do, and yet completely unable to make yourself do it.

That gap, between knowing and doing, is one of the most disorienting parts of ADHD.
And it's exactly where coaching lives.

adhdContent.text: ⚡In an ADHD brain, the wiring between thinking and doing is frayed. Sometimes the spark carries, sometimes not, and rarely is the connection consistent.
⚡It's not a lack of willpower or intelligence. Most certainly not laziness. It’s a bio-mechanical failure in the delivery system of your own intention.
⚡It’s frustrating. It's baffling. It’s invisible.

subsections.note: May wish to add a tooltip for bespoke
subsections.title: ADHD Coaching

defaultContent.text:
ADHD Coaching is a bespoke partnership.
Bespoke, because each person with ADHD experiences different challenges, and to different extents. It focuses on helping you understand better how your brain actually works. And build strategies that work in accordance with it.
A coach is someone who sits beside you on the rug. Not to build it for you. Not to tell you how to build it. To help you figure out what will help move you forward, and keep building.

And over time, as awareness and understanding deepen, you start to notice and strategize by yourself. That shift — from coach-assisted to increasingly self-directed — is the whole point. The coaching relationship is designed to make itself less necessary over time.

adhdContent.text: ↔️ ADHD coaching closes the gap between knowing what to do and doing it — the defining frustration of ADHD.
🪡 It is a custom-tailored partnership because each ADHD brain actually works differently and presents different challenges.
🔍 Over time, coaching builds self-directed awareness — you start catching patterns earlier, needing less external scaffolding.

subsections.note: May wish to add a tooltip for reflective-inquiry
subsections.title: A Different Kind of Support

defaultContent.text: Coaching works alongside other forms of ADHD support such as medication, therapy, or productivity systems. It does not replace them.

Medication addresses the neurobiology directly. It clears the floor so you can begin to build. It does not teach you how to proceed next. Therapy works on emotional wounds and challenges that often travel alongside ADHD. Productivity systems or life hacks often invite you to adapt your brain to someone else's methods. They tell you how to build, the way they did.

Traditional life coaching uses a similar reflective process — however, ADHD coaching is rooted in the neurobiology of the ADHD brain. It acknowledges the ADHD brain is fundamentally different in its operation of regions that affect motivation, focus, and self-regulation.

adhdContent.text: ◾Medication addresses neurobiology
◾Therapy heals emotional wounds
◾Productivity tools invite your brain to use particular techniques
◾ADHD Coaching is grounded in ADHD brain science and focuses on execution.

subsections.note: Standard subsection — bullets in ADHD mode.
subsections.title: How Coaching Works

defaultContent.text:
Coaching is a conversation. A curious one.
At the start of the journey, there's excavation: mapping patterns, understanding your defaults, discovering strengths you draw on naturally.
Then much of the session lives in questions. This is the part that surprises people. A coach doesn't hand you an action plan or efficiency hacks. The exploration is the work.

Between sessions, there's usually one specific commitment — not a to-do list. One thing, specific and small enough to actually be attempted. And if you don't follow through, no guilt. Just data. You're not expected to get it right. You're expected to stay in the process.

adhdContent.text: The work happens through inquiry, not instructions
The coaching process surfaces your own insight; the coach doesn't had you a plan
Coaching isn't telling you what to do or how to do it. It's helping you discover what will enable you do the work.

subsections.note: ADHD content is an image pair (TheirBuildOrder + MyBuildOrder2) — hardcoded in HTML. adhdContent here is ignored by the renderer. To edit the images or labels, update the HTML directly.
subsections.title: Adapt to the Way Your Brain Works

defaultContent.text:
"Make a list, and set a timer."
Much of the advice ADHD persons receive is built on the premise the problem is effort or organization. It isn't. It's a difference in neurobiology — specifically in the brain regions that govern activation: the ability to engage in tasks without immediate reward.

Research shows the ADHD brain thrives on interest, novelty, challenge, urgency, and passion (abbreviated:INCUP). So, we can be strategic in approaching a task — using brain science, instead of a barrel of caffeine.
You might struggle to follow linear instructions 1 to 12. But if tackling the most intersting part first is what gets you started — that's not cheating. That's strategy.

adhdContent.text: Strategically leverage INCUP: If you need to start with the fun parts first to stay interested, we do that.
The process must fit the person, not the other way around.

subsections.title: Self-Awareness + Self-Compassion Come First

defaultContent.text:
Most people arrive wanting to fix a problem. Or fix themselves. That's not where we start.
We start with understanding. Because when every mistake immediately becomes "what's wrong with me" — you don't get insight. You get noise.

Self-awareness helps you stay curious about what's happening — even when what's happening is frustrating. Self-compassion lets you take risks with new strategies without the weight of judgment, and recover from setbacks with grace.
This isn't self-love in a bumper-sticker kind of way. It's motivation built on earned self-respect.

adhdContent.text: Things will inevitably go wrong. Coaching helps you replace that inner critic with a curious observer

subsections.note: Standard subsection — emoji bullets in ADHD mode (emoji act as the visual marker, CSS hides the default LEGO stud).
subsections.title: What Coaching Can and Can't Do

defaultContent.text: A coach is a precision tool — like a brick separator. It makes the hard parts easier. It doesn't do the snapping for you.

Coaching can help you find a better way to organize the pieces. It won't replace therapy, and it won't replace medication. We can't change the pieces you were given — it's a non-standard set — but we can change the way you arrange them.
What coaching will do is help you figure out an arrangement that actually works — and help you stop waiting for the "right" moment to start.

adhdContent.text: ✅ Helps you find a better way to organize the pieces
✅ Helps you stop waiting for the "right" moment to start
✅ Changes the way you arrange what you've got
❌ Won't replace therapy
❌ Won't replace medication
❌ Can't change the pieces you were given
❌ Can't help you win the lottery

subsections.note: Standard subsection — emoji bullets in ADHD mode (emoji act as the visual marker, CSS hides the default LEGO stud).
subsections.title: When Coaching Makes Sense

defaultContent.text: You're ready when the to-build pile is touching the ceiling and you're tired of just looking at the boxes. What used to bring joy now just brings dread.
You don't need more sets. You need a system to open the ones you already have.
Coaching is for the builder who's done waiting for the "perfect" moment — and is ready to see something on the shelf.

adhdContent.text: 🧱 The to-build pile is touching the ceiling
📦 You're tired of just looking at the boxes
😶 What used to bring joy now just brings dread
🔧 You're not sure you're ready — but you're tired of waiting to feel ready
